title: Exponential grow of leaked mounts when mounting file and parent directory with `shared`
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

Bind mount points created with `-v /myroot/myfile:/root/myfile:ro -v /root:/root,rw,shared` aren't cleaned up when the container finishes and grow exponentially.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Run the following command as root:

    ```
    # podman run -v /myroot/myfile:/root/myfile:ro -v /myroot:/root:rw,shared busybox
    ```

2. Check the mounts:

    ```
    # grep myroot /proc/mounts | sort | uniq -c
      1 /dev/mapper/local-root /myroot/myfile xfs rw,seclabel,relatime,attr2,inode64,logbufs=8,logbsize=64k,sunit=128,swidth=256,noquota 0 0
    ```

3. Run the same command again.

4. Check the mounts again:

    ```
    3 /dev/mapper/local-root /myroot/myfile xfs rw,seclabel,relatime,attr2,inode64,logbufs=8,logbsize=64k,sunit=128,swidth=256,noquota 0 0
   ```

**Describe the results you received:**

The bind mount for `/myroot/myfile` isn't removed when the container terminates, and actually it duplicated each time the same command is executed. For example, after running that command 6 times the number of mount points is 63:

```
# grep myroot /proc/mounts | sort | uniq -c
     63 /dev/mapper/local-root /myroot/myfile xfs rw,seclabel,relatime,attr2,inode64,logbufs=8,logbsize=64k,sunit=128,swidth=256,noquota 0 0
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

Expected the mount points to be removed when the container finishes.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

The `/myroot` directory used in the command above only contains the empty `myfile` file:

```
# mkdir /myroot
# touch /myroot/myfile
```

This only reproduces when both a directory and its parent are mounted as volumes, and when that parent is mounted with the `shared` mount propagation option.

When the number of mount points grows (and it is quick, as it is exponential) it becomes impossible to start/restart systemd services because one of the first things it does is reading `/proc/self/mountinfo`. In my environment, for example, I saw more than 90000 entries in that file, which caused systemd to timeout while reading those files.

Note that using `-v` to mount a file and also the directory that contains it is probably wrong. We shouldn't probably be doing it, but I wanted to share this anyhow in case it is something you want to handle in podman.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
# podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.0.2
API Version:  4.0.2
Go Version:   go1.18.4

Built:      Wed Aug 24 02:18:50 2022
OS/Arch:    linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info`:**


```
# rpm -q podman
podman-4.0.2-8.module+el8.7.0+16431+bb67d067.x86_64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


No

I checked the latest Fedora package (`podman-4.2.0-2.fc36.x86_64`) and the result is the same.

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
I don't think it is Podman fault here, and it is the expected behavior.

When the second `/myroot/myfile` mount is created, then it is propagated to its parent on the host, and the mounts propagated internally by the kernel are not controlled by Podman.

Why are you using `shared` if you don't want the mounts to be propagated?

@giuseppe I am inclined to think that using `shared` in our case isn't necessary, and probably a bug in our code. But anyhow I would expect podman to clean up the mounts that it creates. For example, if I do it manually it works and the mount points aren't duplicated:

```
# mount --bind --make-shared /myroot /yourroot
# mount --bind /myroot/myfile /yourroot/myfile

# grep -E '(my|your)' /proc/mounts
/dev/mapper/local-root /yourroot xfs rw,seclabel,relatime,attr2,inode64,logbufs=8,logbsize=64k,sunit=128,swidth=256,noquota 0 0
/dev/mapper/local-root /yourroot/myfile xfs rw,seclabel,relatime,attr2,inode64,logbufs=8,logbsize=64k,sunit=128,swidth=256,noquota 0 0
/dev/mapper/local-root /myroot/myfile xfs rw,seclabel,relatime,attr2,inode64,logbufs=8,logbsize=64k,sunit=128,swidth=256,noquota 0 0

# umount /yourroot/myfile
# umount /yourroot

# grep -E '(my|your)' /proc/mounts
```

After the manual umount there are no leftovers. What is the different between that and and what podman is doing?
podman doesn't do any umount.  It is performed in a new `mount` namespace that is cleaned up when all the processes there are terminated
I see, thanks @giuseppe. I also see that docker does exactly the same. Feel free to close this issue.
